About Whether ChatGPT / AI Will Replace Engineers

This was originally a Facebook page post, but I wanted to keep a longer record, so I converted it into an article. The content is slightly tweaked and a bit more substantial (since it's becoming a post).
About whether ChatGPT / AI will replace engineers
I've been getting a lot of DMs asking whether OpenAI's ChatGPT will replace engineers, so today I'll just share my take in a long-form post.
For now, my view on ChatGPT is...
Setting aside whether it'll replace engineers, let me talk about other professions first. Since I'm an engineer, I asked some friends in other fields to share their ChatGPT experience.

I personally don't think it'll replace other professions in the short term, but to verify how capable ChatGPT is, I asked friends in customer service, law, accounting, etc. — different domains — to try it. Their answers were roughly:
This tool isn't as great as people think, but it's convenient for getting started
Looking up small bits of knowledge is decent
And eventually they shared a common takeaway:

"What ChatGPT outputs isn't always correct — you still need baseline knowledge to judge whether what it gave you is right. But for a chat bot, it's already pretty impressive! It saved me a lot of starting work."

A few said the accuracy rate was around 20%. After discussing this with friends, we agreed that's actually pretty high — so we might be able to look forward to ChatGPT giving even more accurate answers in the future.
Back to just engineers — before ChatGPT got popular, I was already using GitHub Copilot in my own dev and writing.
Do you know what GitHub Copilot is?
GitHub Copilot is an AI tool built jointly by GitHub and OpenAI. ChatGPT is OpenAI's chat bot. So long before ChatGPT got hot, engineers like me were already using similar tools.
Did it put me out of work? No. What I got out of using it was:
"My dev productivity went up! But you need a baseline of knowledge — otherwise Copilot's output drags your code into a worse state than what you'd have written."

Why? Because Copilot sometimes spits out really weird code. You then rely on your Code Review skill — being able to read code — to judge whether what Copilot produced is correct. If not, you have to guide it and correct it so Copilot can do better next time.
GitHub Copilot does analyze your code, but you still can't skip Code Review. So if you were hoping to coast as a salary thief by typing a comment, that's not happening for a while (lol).

So I think one of the things that gets stronger at the end of an engineer's path is Code Review, or Code Review becomes a required engineer skill. Because we keep correcting Copilot's output to make it better.

Copilot launched around 2022, so it's been a while. If you can read or find this post, you're probably still in engineering or in the middle of a career switch — drawn by the "Will ChatGPT / AI replace engineers" topic.
Why? Because if engineers really were replaced, you wouldn't be reading this — you wouldn't be an engineer or pursuing engineering anymore.
So with Copilot, my take is it saves me time writing repetitive code, letting me focus on more complex business logic. Same view for ChatGPT — it's just a very useful "
" to me. Some professions might really get replaced in the future, but not in the short term, because there are still a lot of unresolved controversies — they just haven't surfaced yet.
Anything that touches "money" or "profit" eventually attracts people digging into the data source and training methods — those are unexploded ordnance, just not detonated yet.

So I think we should be thinking about how to integrate this thing into our work, so it boosts our productivity and reduces repetitive, low-value work. That's the direction worth thinking and worrying about. We may write a lot less code in the future, but we also need to think about how to use these tools to increase our own value.

Maybe new roles will emerge — Prompt engineer, for example (someone who specializes in writing prompts to guide AI to produce correct output). But how to actually write good prompts is its own skill. If everyone knew how to write prompts that guide AI to correct outputs, I wouldn't have written "

Hope I've taken at least some of the worry out of the "Will ChatGPT / AI replace engineers" question.
Personally I'm happy about AI's progress — it represents progress for humanity. We're trying to use AI to solve problems and remove repetitive work, so we can focus on more valuable work.
(Of course, I'm also a bit afraid of AI's exponential growth — Terminator movies showed us how that goes XD)
